mcqs.question: What does an Optimal Binary Search Tree (OBST) minimize?

options.text: The expected (weighted) search cost based on access frequencies

mcqs.explanation: An OBST arranges keys so that the expected search cost (sum of access probability times search depth for each key) is minimized. Frequently accessed keys are placed closer to the root.

mcqs.question: Which algorithmic technique is used to construct an Optimal Binary Search Tree?
mcqs.explanation: OBST construction uses dynamic programming because it has optimal substructure (optimal subtrees of an OBST are themselves optimal) and overlapping subproblems.

mcqs.question: In the OBST dynamic programming formulation, what does c[i][j] represent?

options.text: The number of keys between i and j
The minimum cost of a BST containing keys k_i+1 to k_j
The depth of key k_i in the optimal tree
The sum of all key frequencies

mcqs.explanation: c[i][j] stores the minimum expected search cost of an optimal BST formed from the keys k_(i+1) through k_j. The overall answer is c[0][n].

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of the standard dynamic programming algorithm for constructing an OBST with n keys?
mcqs.explanation: The standard DP algorithm uses three nested loops: one for the chain length, one for the starting index, and one to try all possible roots. This gives O(n^3) time. Knuth's optimization can reduce it to O(n^2).

mcqs.question: In the OBST problem, what are dummy keys (or failure nodes)?

options.text: Nodes representing unsuccessful searches falling between or outside the actual keys

mcqs.explanation: Dummy keys (d_0 to d_n) represent search ranges for values not present in the tree. They account for unsuccessful searches and have their own probabilities (q values) in the cost calculation.
